title: Fast Times at Ridgemont High? The Effect of Compulsory Schooling Laws on Teenage Births
design: US state/cohort compulsory-law design; Norwegian reform design
reform: Minimum dropout ages in US; Norway minimum dropout age 14 to 16
schooling_first_stage: Law-based mandatory attainment; exact first-stage coefficient not extracted in current pass
effect_summary: US dropout age 16: -0.008 probability of teen birth (4.7% of mean); dropout age 17: -0.015 (8.8%); Norway reform: -0.006 (3.5%)
persistence_or_rebound: Age-threshold pattern reported; completed-fertility follow-up unavailable
source_locator: PDF abstract; Table 2 discussion, PDF pp. 17-18

title: The Impact of Education on Family Formation: Quasi-Experimental Evidence from the UK
design: Regression discontinuity at 1972 UK ROSLA cohort cutoff
fertility_outcome: Age-specific births at 16-19; completed fertility by 45
effect_summary: Age 17 reduced-form -0.0031 to -0.0034 (3.1-3.4 births per 1,000; about 8%); IV scaling about 26% decline
persistence_or_rebound: No detectable effects at ages 18-19; effects essentially zero beyond teen years
completed_fertility: Precisely zero; 95% CI excludes effects larger than one tenth of OLS association
mechanism: Conception reduction during compulsory schooling; negligible abortion effect; incapacitation
source_locator: PDF introduction p. 3; Table 1 pp. 16-17; Table 3 pp. 20-21

title: Books or babies? The incapacitation effect of schooling on minority women
design: Regression discontinuity around school-entry cutoff and leaving-age reform
schooling_first_stage: Enrollment-rule compliance about 78-80%; reform exposure extends schooling
fertility_outcome: Teenage motherhood; age at motherhood; conception timing
effect_summary: Teenage motherhood decreased 13.4-26.0%; motherhood delayed by 2 years
persistence_or_rebound: Pregnancy falls during school terms but not summer/Christmas breaks
mechanism: Direct incapacitation evidence using conception dates
source_locator: PDF abstract; mechanism discussion; enrollment compliance Table 2

title: Women's education, marriage, and fertility outcomes: Evidence from Thailand's compulsory schooling law
reform: Compulsory education extended from 6 to 9 years
schooling_first_stage: Lower-secondary completion +7.6 to +8.9 pp overall; heterogeneous increases
fertility_outcome: Ever birth and births by age; cumulative births
effect_summary: Ever birth falls about 4-5 pp at ages 14-15; births decrease through age 17
persistence_or_rebound: Fertility rises after school years and is significantly higher at age 20; catch-up around ages 20-21
mechanism: Predominantly in-school incapacitation; short-lived human-capital spillover; delayed marriage
source_locator: PDF abstract; Figure 4 discussion pp. 16-17; Table 1 first stage

title: Do Compulsory Schooling Laws Affect Fertility Behaviors and Marriages? Evidence From India
reform: 2010 Right to Education Act; compulsory primary education ages 6-14
fertility_outcome: Sexual activity, marriage, first birth, and fertility by ages 15-25
effect_summary: Sexual activity by 18 -7.2 pp; married by 21 -3.9 pp; first birth by 21 -2.6 pp
persistence_or_rebound: Fertility effect becomes more negative in early twenties rather than disappearing after school
mechanism: Human capital/knowledge, contraception, partner matching; paper rejects incapacitation as main channel
source_locator: PDF abstract; Table 3 p. 14; Table 4 pp. 15-16; conclusion p. 23

title: Compulsory Education and Teenage Motherhood
design: Sharp regression discontinuity at UK ROSLA cohort cutoff
reform: Minimum school-leaving age increased by one year
schooling_first_stage: School participation falls about 30 pp at cutoff; reform increases mandatory schooling
fertility_outcome: Motherhood at each teenage age and cumulative teenage motherhood
effect_summary: Motherhood at age 16 -0.40 pp; cumulative effect by age 18 -0.81 pp
persistence_or_rebound: Non-monotonic teen pattern; positive effect at 19; reform influence dissipates after age 20
source_locator: PDF abstract; Tables 2-3 and discussion pp. 20-23

title: The Effects of Compulsory-Schooling Laws on Teenage Marriage and Births in Turkey
reform: Compulsory schooling extended from 5 to 8 years in 1997
schooling_first_stage: Large enrollment increase in targeted ages; exact coefficient pending table extraction
fertility_outcome: Marriage by 16 and first birth by 17-18
effect_summary: Marriage by 16 reduced 44%; first birth by 17 reduced 36%
persistence_or_rebound: Effects disappear after age 17 for marriage and age 18 for first birth in published version; delay operates through marriage
mechanism: Compulsory attendance plus temporary marriage-market human-capital effect
source_locator: IZA PDF abstract; Tables 5-8; conclusion pp. 27-28

title: Does education reduce teen fertility? Evidence from compulsory schooling laws
reform: Changes in provincial minimum school-leaving ages
schooling_first_stage: Strong law-induced schooling first stage; dropout-age increase raises attainment about 6-7 pp on attendance margin
fertility_outcome: First birth and number of children by ages 17 onward
effect_summary: Teen birth probability reduced about 2-3 pp; effects concentrated at ages 17-18
persistence_or_rebound: Negative effect disappears after ages 17-18; temporary delay

title: The Causal Effects of Education on Age at Marriage and Marital Fertility
design: Regression discontinuity at 1947 and 1972 English school-leaving reforms
reform: Minimum school-leaving age raised by one year in 1947 and 1972
schooling_first_stage: Reform-scaled additional year; exact preferred first-stage coefficient not extracted here
fertility_outcome: First-marriage fertility; age at first marriage
effect_summary: 1947 scaled fertility effect range -0.046 to +0.132 child; 1972 range -0.030 to +0.061; preferred estimates effectively zero
completed_fertility: Precisely zero/non-trivial negative effects ruled out
mechanism: Prospective woman's own education; no child-work-value test
source_locator: PDF Tables A12-A13 and Figure 6 discussion pp. 18-20; conclusion p. 23

title: Is Education Always Reducing Fertility? Evidence from Compulsory Schooling Reforms
setting: England and seven Continental European countries
schooling_first_stage: Mandatory schooling instruments; single-country first stages generally 0.22-0.40 years
fertility_outcome: Completed number of biological children and childlessness
effect_summary: Negative education-fertility effect in England; no fertility reduction or childlessness increase in Continental Europe
mechanism: Prospective adult's own schooling; marriage-market and labor-supply channels
source_locator: Author manuscript abstract and Sections 2-4; completed-fertility definition pp. 11-12

title: Maternal education and child health: Causal evidence from Denmark
reform: Compulsory schooling raised from 7 to 9 years in 1972
schooling_first_stage: Strong compulsory-margin first stage; preferred first-stage F about 118
fertility_outcome: Number of children, any child, and age at first birth
effect_summary: No effect on having children or number of children; age at first birth postponed about 0.5 year, marginally significant
persistence_or_rebound: Completed number of children measured at age 39
completed_fertility: No causal effect on number of children
mechanism: Prospective mother's own schooling; timing effect not specifically teenage incapacitation
source_locator: PDF Table 1; Tables 2-3 first stage; Table 6 and discussion

title: The effect of schooling on teenage childbearing: evidence using changes in compulsory education laws
reform: Minimum leaving age 14 to 15 and 15 to 16
effect_summary: Post-contraception IV estimates imply reductions of 1.0 pp by age 17 and 4.0 pp by age 20 in the preferred polynomial specification
mechanism: Own schooling; estimates do not isolate pure attendance from human-capital channels

title: The effect of female education on fertility: Evidence from China's compulsory schooling reform
fertility_outcome: Total live births and childlessness among married women age 35+
effect_summary: An additional year of female education reduces total live births by 0.238 (SE 0.059); no significant childlessness effect
mechanism: Delayed marriage and childbirth, opportunity cost, assortative mating, and child mortality; not reduced value of children's work
source_locator: Published PDF Table 3 and mechanism section

title: The Effects of Education on Fertility: Evidence from Taiwan
reform: 1968 extension of compulsory education from 6 to 9 years
fertility_outcome: Fertility status in 1980 and children ever born in 2010
effect_summary: Exact-date RD estimates show no effect of the reform-induced increase in women's education on fertility
mechanism: Prospective woman's own schooling; labor-market and marriage outcomes checked, but child-work value not tested

title: Education, desired fertility, and HIV/AIDS: Evidence from China's compulsory schooling law
schooling_first_stage: Each exposure year raises schooling by about 0.37-0.45 years in survey samples
fertility_outcome: Desired number of children and contraceptive behavior
effect_summary: One additional year of law exposure reduces women's desired number of children by 0.009, with no detectable effect for men
mechanism: Women's opportunity cost and intrahousehold contraceptive bargaining, not children's work value
source_locator: Published PDF abstract; Tables 3 and 5; fertility-preference section
